I recall you asked about this earlier. I've looked through the OpenSSL code, and the logic around MTU determination sure has changed a lot. I think that just setting this to the 802.3 default won't work, though: there can be many reasons that path MTU might be lower than this. One common scenario is DSL: PPPoE induces an 8-byte overhead, reducing the MTU to 1492 right at the network border. I think that PyDTLS would just fail now with this hardcode in such situations.

In bss_dgram.c I see that on Linux, OpenSSL tries to determine the MTU from sockopt IP_MTU as part of BIO_CTRL_DGRAM_QUERY_MTU. On Windows, this path is not taken, and this query just returns 0. The caller then falls into a different code path, which computes a minimum by subtracting a protocol-dependent overhead from a guess.

So I think we need to trace though this and find out why that's not working on Windows. Would you like to do that with a debug version of the OpenSSL dll's you built?

Yes, it depends. _For that use case_ of simply retrying a connection for any type of failure, I agree that it's more convenient to catch one exception than two. However some clients may want to behave differently depending on the type of error.

For example, if the server actively refuses a connection it may be because Rabbit isn't running; whereas if the socket can't be opened it more likely indicates a local failure. By shoving both errors into one exception, we're ruling out the possibility of clients distinguishing the two types of failure; plus it's hard to go back to two exceptions because of backwards compatibility.

The `from e` trick does partially address this loss of information, but it's more designed for debugging than error handling.

If a client really does want to retry on _all_ failures, you can just catch any type of exception (`except Exception as e`).

@reminisce It's a good point regarding the Module. Unfortunately this initial PR does not support Module, partly because it relies on Symbol's `simple_bind` method to provide the shared buffer, which is necessary because TensorRT stores copies of the weights. Binding from Module hides away the access to the weights from the user, and the user providing the weights during binding makes this approach work not only with symbolic models, but also Gluon models converted to symbolic from the pre-trained GluonCV model zoo. The concern over Module is valid, and the support for it would probably require some eventual re-working on the integration, but I though it would be good to at least get this initial integration out there, and then broaden the scope to the more common Module approach.

It doesn't need , actually.  These are for the "top level" of your query.  You COULD include it, technically.  But that would only be if you were going to query for just the ``options`` by themselves.  However, for us, that wouldn't be useful.  It would just show you the option ``name`` and ``id``.  

What we care about is the mapping of ``optionCollectionHash`` to ``options``.  So the ``resolve_all_option_collections`` handles that.  It will give you each ``optionCollection`` with the ``options`` that apply to it.

    type(service_name + 'RequestBuilder', bases, dct)

Where `dct` is the dictionary from method name to `create_method`-generated methods. You still get the same behavior of passing all calls through `make_request` but you're now generating a separate class for the service instead of dynamic `setattr` like that (and it now also inherits the service Iface class). You could theoretically even use `slots` or named tuple with this approach if you wanted to.

Now I'm really confused :wink:. Here's my idealized picture of how a symmetric 2D blob should work:

1. The Gaussian blob is a continuous function defined on (real numbers)^2, centered on an arbitrary real-valued location $x, y$. It has an Rg of `SIZE` units.
- That function is discretized. In a camera, each pixel is a (possibly weighted) average of the function over a certain region. For the artificial features, right now we sample the function at a single point, presumably the center of the pixel.
- The `locate` function tries to recover x, y, and Rg.

How is this different from what we are doing now? Is the problem at step 2? Are the various `+ 1` and `- 1` attempts to compensate for that?

What really troubles me is that the correction of 0.2 is approximately the same for different sizes. As the Gaussian gets larger (and therefore smoother), the details of the discretization should matter less and less. So why is it constant?

While this is definitely nicer for the developer than what I'm about to propose, I think we should remove `testfixtures` from the project. Many of our package maintainers use our unit tests to check if Certbot is working properly on their system. When they run these tests, they do so without our `dev` dependencies meaning this test would fail for them. We could add `testfixtures` as a regular dependency but that is a step backwards on #1301 and requires all OS's to have a `testfixtures` package available.

Instead, I think we should just use `mock` here. You can probably register logging handler and check output that way, but for this test you can do something like:
```
        with mock.patch("certbot_nginx.configurator.logger") as mock_logger:
            self.config.enhance("geese.com", "redirect")
        self.assertEqual(mock_logger.info.call_args[0][0],
                'No matching insecure server blocks listening on port %s found.')
```
Note the need to use `%s` rather than the port number.

I asked @ohemorange about this because I think she has a stronger opinion here than I do and she has a strong preference for us only taking `--cert-name`. Her main argument was we should limit the number of ways there are to accomplish a task in Certbot. This makes both the code and documentation simpler.

If we take `--domains`, we'll need a way to get the chain path in the future. With the way this code is now, if you request `--staple-ocsp`, it will error and say that `--chain-path` needs to be provided, but even if you provide it, we'll still error because it's not being passed through to `enhance_config`. We could fix that by providing the value of `chain_path` from the `config`, but we might instead want to try and find the matching certificate for the given domains. Doing this opens a massive cans of worms which I don't think we want.

I don't have that strong of an opinion, so you and Erica can debate it if you want, but I'll say my preference would be to err on the side of providing fewer bells and whistles initially by not supporting `--domains` since support can always be added later if there is significant demand/need for the feature.

## Why allow the return of "afterbacks"?

This is what enables redirects.

A `Redirect` is essentially a before/afterback (b/a) pair wrapped in one object:

1. `Redirect.__init__` : Gathers the `target` b/a, calls the beforeback, and creates a closure that holds the beforeback's result and will execute the afterback when called.
2. `Redirect.__call__`: triggers the aforementioned closure, which runs the afterback.

Because callable objects returned by beforebacks act like a afterbacks. A "redirection" of a notification can be performed by returning a `Redirect` from a beforeback.

Similarly, to my previous comment, I'm aware that some people feel comments should be removed except where absolutely needed (in favour of self-documenting code), I personally think it's usually better to err on the side of more comments.

People reading our code are usually less knowledgeable than we would think they are, and I'd rather not only write code for "experience Python devs" or "experience Flask devs" or whatever.

So in this case, unless you feel very strongly about it, I'd rather keep the comment.

This sort of leads to a principle I have that I'd like on the whole to keep our criticism of pull-requests to things with significant impact, so we don't spend a lot of time bike-shedding, and examples of such things to me include discussions about nuanced code style, strict opinions about commit messages, etc.

However this is just my opinions and If you want to have a wider discussion about this, and the previous item, please go ahead and suggest a PR on [the practices repo](https://github.com/canonical-webteam/practices) and it will start a discussion with the whole team and we can come to a consensus.
